segments.s366-g241: The phrase is ordered like a small clause: causal adverbial + possessor + verb + object. از اندیشه دل is a causal/agent phrase ('because of thought' or 'from thinking, in his heart'); نیامدش is the past negative نیامد (did not come) with the enclitic -ش (to him/it), so خواب is the thing that did not come. Literally: 'From thought the heart, sleep did not come to him.'
segments.s366-g242: This clause uses repeated preposition به for parallel complements ('to/for battle and to/for his feast'). بزمش has the genitive/enclitic -ش 'his' attached. گرفته is the past participle/compound use of گرفتن meaning 'seized/taken,' and شتاب is the noun 'haste' — together conveying 'haste has seized him' or 'he was seized by haste' (the affected person is indicated by the clitic on بزمش).
segments.s366-g105: بیاید is a 3rd-person singular narrative/present verb 'comes'; the verb precedes its subject in this elevated/poetic word order. پس is an adverb 'then' modifying the action. آن نره شیر دلیر is a noun phrase: آن (that) + نره شیر (literally 'male lion', a compound epithet) with the adjective دلیر ('brave') following the head, so the whole phrase is the subject that the verb 바이ad refers to.
segments.s366-g106: سوار دلاور is an appositive noun phrase ('the valiant rider') that further describes the subject; there is no overt copula. که introduces a relative clause 'who/whose', and نامش contains نام ('name') plus the pronominal clitic -ش ('his'), so 'whose name is'. The copula 'is' is omitted (zero copula) as normal in Persian.
segments.s366-g107: به پیش اندر آید is a locative phrase plus verb meaning 'comes forward' or 'approaches' (به + پیش + اندر + آید). گرفته is a past participle 'having taken/seized' and is used here adverbially to show accompanying action; کمند ('lasso') is its object, so 'having taken a lasso'.
segments.s366-g108: نشسته is a past participle functioning as an adjective 'seated'. بر introduces the location ('on/upon'), اسفندیاری is an attributive form relating to Esfandiyār ('Esfandiyār’s' or 'pertaining to Esfandiyār'), and سمند is the noun 'steed' — together 'seated on Esfandiyār’s steed.'
segments.s366-g109: ابا here is orthographically a form of با ('with') used in poetry (instrumental 'with'); the sequence جوشن زر درخشان is a noun phrase 'a cuirass of gold, shining' and چو ماه is a simile 'like the moon'. Together: 'with a shining golden cuirass like the moon.'
segments.s366-g110: بدو is the contraction of به + او ('to/at him') functioning as a prepositional object 'toward him' or 'at him'. اندرون indicates 'within/inside', خیره گشته is a compound participial adjectival phrase 'become astonished/stunned' (خیره 'astonished' + گشته 'has become'), and سپاه is the noun 'army' being described: 'the army inside became astonished at him.'
segments.s366-g111: بگیرد is a 3rd-person singular present narrative verb 'seizes/takes'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and introduces the source phrase گردان لشکر هزار ('a division/troop of a thousand soldiers' — genitive sequence where گردان modifies لشکر هزار). The phrase means 'takes from the ranks/rotation of a thousand soldiers.'
segments.s366-g112: A sequence of verbs in narrative present: ببندد 'he binds' (transitive), فرستد 'he sends/dispatches' (here with deliberate sense 'dispatches'), followed by the prepositional phrase بر شهریار ('to the king') where بر functions as 'to/toward' and شهریار is 'king'. The object of the verbs is implied from the previous clause (the seized troops).
segments.s366-g153: This is a noun phrase listing the sources of light: فروغ (radiance) followed by the items that possess or emit it. Persian poetry often omits the linking ezāfe (‑e) here, so فروغِ سرِ نیزه و تیر و تیغ reads as “the radiance of the spear‑head, arrow, and sword.” The word order (head noun before its modifiers) is idiomatic and compact for verse.
segments.s366-g154: بتابد is a 3rd‑person singular verbal form meaning “(it) shines/gleams” (classical/poetic present). چنان چون is a strengthened comparative (“so/just as”), followed by ستاره “star.” ز is the poetic preposition meaning “from,” and میغ means “blade/edge.” Together: “(it) shines thus, like a star from the blade.”
segments.s366-g155: وزان is the conjunctional/relational particle و + ز آن, linking this line to what preceded (“and from that/and from those”). زخم مردان is a noun phrase meaning “the wounds of/inflicted on men” (in context: wounds dealt in battle). کجا here is a rhetorical ‘where’ (introducing the place/spot of striking). می‌زنند is the 3rd‑person plural present of زدن meaning “to strike/inflict” (they strike/they give).
segments.s366-g156: This clause describes the result: و begins the clause (“and”), بر یکدگر is the prepositional phrase “upon one another,” and بر همی افگند is a poetic verbal phrase: همی is an archaic/emphatic adverb (here reinforcing continuous or emphatic action) and افگند (from افکندن) is a perfect/past form meaning “threw/cast” used in epic narration. Together: “and they cast/threw themselves upon one another.”
segments.s366-g157: همه begins the clause meaning “all/everyone.” خسته و کشته are two coordinated adjectives/participial states: “weary” and “killed/slain.” بر یکدگر is the prepositional phrase “upon one another,” indicating how the bodies lie — a compact, paratactic description typical of epic verse.
segments.s366-g158: This is a terse, repetitive listing that intensifies the tragedy: each noun is paired with بر (“upon”), so پسر بر پدر بر پدر بر پسر reads literally “son upon father, father upon son.” The repeated preposition and parallel structure are rhetorical/poetic devices rather than full clauses.
segments.s366-g159: وزان again links to what came before (“and from that/therefrom”). ناله و زاری is a coordinated noun phrase meaning “groan(s) and lamentation(s)/wailing.” خستگان is the plural noun “the weary” (those exhausted), so the whole phrase: “from those groans and wailings of the weary.”
segments.s366-g160: به + بند اندر آیند is a verbal phrase: به here means “into/toward,” بند is a poetic noun meaning “bond/fetter” (and also can mean ‘stanza’ but here it denotes being bound), اندر آیند (present plural of آمدن with اندر = “into”) means “they come/are brought into.” نابستگان is a substantive (literally “the unbound” or outsiders); in context it refers to those who end up taken or brought into bondage. The line says, in effect, “the unbound/helpless are led into fetters.”
segments.s366-g81: This is a nominal+verbal clause: بسی (intensive adverb) modifies رنج (suffering), and بیند is the classical third-person singular present/subjunctive of دیدن used here in the sense 'meet/experience' (i.e. 'he experiences'). به رزم اندرون is a prepositional phrase 'in/at the inner battle' with به + noun + اندرون (inside). Word order places the adverbial phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s366-g82: شه خسروان را بگویم is first-person statement 'I will tell the king of kings' (شه خسروان = 'king of kings'); را is the definite/object marker attached to the object. بگویم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive of گفتن used here as 'I will say/tell'. که چون introduces the content clause 'how/that', with چون functioning as 'how' (introducing the manner clause).
segments.s366-g83: A noun phrase: درفش is the head 'banner/standard', modified by the adjective فروزنده 'radiant/illuminating' and followed by the genitive/attributive کاویان 'of the Kayanids'. The order is noun + adjective + genitive, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s366-g84: بیفگنده باشند is a jussive/subjunctive-style verb cluster expressing wish or possibility ('may have set/hoisted' or 'may remain set'); باشـند is the subjunctive/optative form 'may be' and ایرانیان is the subject placed after the verb for emphasis ('the Iranians'). In classical Persian such verb-subject order with باشنـد expresses a hope or exhortation.
segments.s366-g85: گرامی is an adjective 'honored/precious' qualifying the implied banner; بگیرد is 3rd-person present/subjunctive of گرفتن 'to seize/take' (here 'he seized'), and به دندان is a prepositional phrase 'with his teeth' indicating instrument. The direct object درفش appears after the instrument, a poetic word order.
segments.s366-g86: This repeats the same basic action with slight variation: به دندان 'with his teeth' + بدارد (here 'he holds' or 'he keeps') + درفش بنفش 'the purple banner'. بدارد is a present-tense verb meaning 'he has/holds' and بنفش is a postpositive adjective 'purple' following درفش.
segments.s366-g87: به یک دست شمشیر و دیگر کلاه is an instrumental/ablative-like phrase 'with a sword in one hand and a helmet in the other'. به + [دست] indicates placement/instrument; دیگر 'the other' pairs with کلاه 'helmet' to show the items in the two hands. The ordering places the two comitative instruments before the subject-verb phrase.
segments.s366-g88: به دندان درفش is a prepositional phrase 'the banner in (his) teeth' placed before the subject; فریدون شاه ('Fereydun the king') is the nominative subject coming last for emphasis. The name + title order is a standard poetic way to name the agent.
segments.s366-g25: This is a conditional circumstantial clause. مرا (mara) here marks possession in an archaic existential/possessive pattern (literally: 'to me there was…'); گر + نبودی is the classical conditional 'if ... had not been' (نبودی = past/subjunctive of بودن). خرد شهریار is a noun phrase 'the kingly / sovereign wisdom' that is the thing 'to me' whose existence is being denied in the protasis.
segments.s366-g26: نکردی is the 2nd-person past negative 'you did not make / cause'. ز (poetic for از) = 'of / from', followed by من 'me' as the internal argument. بودنی is the deverbal noun/adjective 'being, existence' (the -ی nominalizer), and خواستار is a predicative adjective 'desirous, petitioner'. Together زِ منِ بودنی خواستار = 'desirous of my being' (object/result of نکردی).
segments.s366-g27: مگر here functions as 'unless' (introducing the exception/condition). با من = 'with me' (prepositional complement). از داد = 'of/from justice' (a genitive/qualifying phrase: 'a covenant from/grounded in justice'). پیمان کند uses کند as the classical present/subjunctive/optative 3rd-person 'let him make / may he make' (or in narrative: 'he makes').
segments.s366-g28: که introduces a content clause 'that / such that'. نه … نه sets up a binary negative 'neither ... nor ...'. بد کند = 'do bad/evil' (بد as adjective/adverb, کند = 3rd-sg do); خود نه فرمان کند = 'nor (by) himself command' (خود = emphatic 'himself/ personally'; فرمان = 'command/order'; final کند repeats the 3rd-sg verb). The whole clause states terms of the covenant: he will neither do evil himself nor order it.
segments.s366-g29: جهانجوی is the speaker's name/title and functions as the subject/vocative. گفتا is the archaic past form 'said' (classical past). به نام خدای is the usual formula 'in the name of God' (به = 'in/by', نام = 'name', خدای = 'of God').
segments.s366-g30: بدین is the fused/prepositional form (به + این) meaning 'with this / by this'. و is the conjunction 'and'. به دین آور is an imperative/optative instruction 'bring/produce into the faith' (به دین = 'into the religion/faith', آور = 'bring'). پاک رای is a noun phrase 'pure counsel / pure opinion' (پاک qualifying رای). Together it is an instruction: 'with this, and in the faith bring a pure counsel.'
segments.s366-g31: به جان زریر is an oath-formula 'by the life/soul of Zarir' (به = 'by', جان = 'life/soul', زریر = proper name). آن نبرده سوار is a verbless-ordering found in epic style where آن (that) + نبرده (has not taken/borne — negative past participle/compound) + سوار (rider) yields 'that rider has not taken (him / it)' — نبرده is the negative perfect/past form meaning 'has not taken'.
segments.s366-g32: به جان گرانمایه اسفندیار is a second oath-formula 'by the precious life of Esfandiyar' (same structure as the previous: به جان = 'by the life of', گرانمایه = 'precious/esteemed' modifying the name اسفندیار). It stands alone as an emphatic oath or invocation.
segments.s366-g97: چو + present verb آید forms a temporal clause 'when ... comes'. سرانجام is an adverb 'at last/finally' modifying the clause. پیروز is the nominative noun 'the victor' and باز is an adverb 'again' — the whole segment = 'when at last the victor returns/comes again.'
segments.s366-g98: ابر دشمنان is a poetic apposition/collective 'a cloud (ابر) of enemies (دشمنان)'. دست کرده دراز is a verbal/participial phrase: دست کرده = having made/put forth the hand and دراز = 'long/extended', so together 'stretch out (their) hands.' کردۀ is the perfect/participial use of کردن attached to دست.
segments.s366-g99: بیاید here is a narrative/present-formation meaning 'appears / comes (may come)'. پس is a connective adverb 'then/thereafter'. آن برگزیده سوار is a noun phrase 'that chosen rider' (آن + adjective برگزیده + head noun سوار).
segments.s366-g100: پس again functions as a discourse adverb 'then'. شهریار is the head noun 'king/lord'. جهان نامدار is an epithet 'world-renowned' (جهان + adjective نامدار) modifying شهریار: 'the world's famed king.' Word order places the epithet after جهان as a poetic compound.
segments.s366-g101: ز is the poetic ablative 'from/of'. آهرمنان (Ahrimans) names the hostile/demonic foes. بفگند is the 3rd-person singular present of افکندن 'to cast/throw' used here in an epic, simple tense. شست گرد is an archaic/poetic phrase: شست (here part of a compound verbal phrase) + گرد ('around') — together indicating a turning or sweeping motion of the hand; the phrasing is compact and somewhat idiomatic in epic Persian.
segments.s366-g102: نماید is 3rd-person singular of نمودن 'to show, to make appear' and here means 'one shows/displays or turns'. یکی is the indefinite 'one / a certain one'. پهلوی دستبرد is a genitive/compound meaning 'the flank/side for a (forceful) seizure/encroachment' (پهلوی = flank, دستبرد = seizure/attack). The whole segment describes someone exposing a flank for attack.
segments.s366-g103: سرانجام is the temporal adverb 'at last/finally'. ترکان is the subject 'the Turks'. به + تیرش here marks the instrument/means 'with arrows' (تیر with a possessive suffix -ش that in context functions generically as 'with arrows'). زنند is the 3rd-person plural present/post-classical form of زدن 'to strike/shoot' (the entry was split into زن + ند; زن is the root 'strike' and ند is the plural verb ending, which in the list is not used as a lexical sense here).
segments.s366-g104: تن is 'body'. پیلوارش is an adjectival/possessive compound (پیلوار + -ش) meaning something like 'his successive/continuous (body)' — poetically describing the body as a single, sustained object; پیلوار is a poetic adjective 'continuous/successive'. به خاک افگنند is 'they throw/cast to the ground' (افگنند = they cast/throw). The whole clause: 'they cast his body to the earth.'
segments.s366-g217: A compound nominative phrase: کیان زادگان is a genitive/attributive pairing meaning 'those born of the Kians / royal-born', followed by و جوانان 'and the young men' and the possessive enclitic ش (written here as من after the noun sequence) marking possession ('my'). Word order is noun + modifier + conjunction + noun + possessor (typical Persian noun phrases).
segments.s366-g218: A relative/qualifying clause introduced by که 'who/that'. هر یک 'each one' is the subject; چنانند is a 3rd‑person plural predicate ('are such / resemble'), agreeing with the plural subject; چون introduces a simile 'like/as' and جان من is the object of comparison 'my life/soul' (جان with the possessive من).
segments.s366-g219: بخوانم is a 1st‑person singular present/future/jussive form 'I will call/summon' (classical narrative uses present for immediate action); همه سربسر is an emphatic adverbial 'everyone, one and all' (همه 'all' + سربسر 'entirely/without exception'); پیش خویش is a prepositional phrase 'before myself / in my presence' where خوی + ش forms the reflexive/possessive 'خویش' (the segmentation shows the enclitic ش).
segments.s366-g220: This line strings short clauses: زره‌شان 'their armor' (زره + possessive ش), followed by the negated verb نپوشم. Here نپوشم is 1st‑person singular negative subjunctive/imperative form 'I will not put on / (I will not let them) wear' — in context it functions as 'I will not allow them (to) wear their armor' or 'I will not have them don their armor'. نشانم is نشان + ـم (possessive 'my sign' / 'my emblem'); به پیش 'before/in front (of them or me)' places that emblem forward. Word order is object + verb + (possessive + locative), typical in poetic Persian.
segments.s366-g221: A rhetorical interrogative: چگونه 'how' introduces the question; رسد is 3rd‑person singular present/future 'reaches / can reach' (used here hypothetically); نوک تیر خدنگ is a noun phrase 'the tip (نوک) of an arrow (تیر) described as خدنگ 'dagger-like' (a compound attributive).
segments.s366-g222: برین آسمان is best read as بر + ین + آسمان, an archaic/poetic formation meaning 'that lofty/high sky' (برin as intensive/adjectival formation + آسمان). بر شده here is a verbal/adjectival phrase 'having been raised / standing upon' (بر + شده), followed by کوه سنگ 'a mountain of stone' (کوه + سنگ). The phrase pictures the sky as high and the rock‑mountain as set upon/against it.
segments.s366-g223: A simple reporting clause: خردمند 'the wise man' is the subject; گفتا is an archaic past/quotative form of گفت 'said' (classical narrative past); به شاه زمین is the indirect object phrase 'to the king of the land' (به + شاه + زمین). Word order Subject - Verb - indirect object is normal in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s366-g224: An address/vocative phrase. که‌ای here introduces the direct address (classical vocative particle + pronoun-like element); نیک‌خو 'kind‑natured / virtuous' and مهتر 'chief / eminent elder' are epithets; بافرین is an adjectival/epithetical form meaning 'praiseworthy / glorious' (the whole sequence is a formal poetic salutation directed at the king).
segments.s366-g113: This clause is a prepositional phrase + question-word + verb phrase in fronted order: به هر سو ('to/toward every side') gives direction (به = towards, هر سو = every direction). کجا بنهد is a relative question-like construction 'where he sets/places' with بنهد (verb nهاد in present/subjunctive-ish form 'places/sets') governing آن شاه روی as its object: آن شاه ('that/the king') + روی ('face/visage'). The literal order is 'to every side where sets that king (his) face' → 'wherever that king turned his face.'
segments.s366-g114: همی is a classical durative/continuous particle ('ever/constantly') placed before the verb. راند is the verbal root (drive/ride/pursue) in narrative present. از introduces the goal or object related to جوی here; خون بدخواه جوی is a noun-phrase 'seeking (جوی) the blood (خون) of the ill-wisher/enemy (بدخواه)'. Grammatically the clause describes an ongoing action: 'he constantly rides/drives, seeking the enemy's blood.'
segments.s366-g115: نه is the sentential negator 'not/no', modifying the following verb. استد here functions as a past narrative verb ('stood/was' in older usage); کس after the negator yields 'no one' (کس = no one here). آن پهلوان شاه را is the noun phrase being referred to ('that champion-king'), with را marking it as the (definite/emphatic) object/topic. The whole means 'no one stood/was against that champion-king.'
segments.s366-g116: ستوه آورد is a causative verbal phrase: ستوه ('weary') is used as a predicate/adjectival complement and آورد ('caused, brought about') is the verb 'to cause'. The subject (understood from context) is شاه ('the king'), and خرگاه را is the definite object marked by را. So syntactically it reads '[he] caused the donkey-stall (خرگاه را) to be weary' — i.e. 'the king wearied the stable/low place.'
segments.s366-g117: پس is a temporal connector 'then'. افگنده is a past/perfect participle 'cast down/struck' placed before the verb (a common poetic order). بیند is the narrative-present form 'he sees'. بزرگ اردشیر is the noun phrase 'the great Ardashir' that is modified by افگنده: literally 'then he sees great Ardashir cast down.'
segments.s366-g118: سیه گشته is a compound participial expression: سیه ('black/dark') + گشته (perfect participle 'become/turned'), i.e. 'having become black/blackened'. رخسار و تن are the two coordinated affected nouns 'countenance (cheek) and body'. چون + زریر is a simile 'like Zarihr' (چون = 'like/as'). Together: 'his countenance and body blackened like Zarihr's.'
segments.s366-g119: This clause strings a sequence of short verbal phrases. بگرید is the classical narrative/present-form of 'to weep' ('he wept/was weeping'). برو is the verb 'go' (here 'went off'), modified by the adjective زار ('wretched/grief-stricken') as complement. و links the clauses, and گردد (from گشتن) is used causatively/state-of-being 'becomes', taking نژند ('base/abased') as predicative complement. So: 'He wept, went away wretched, and became abased.'
segments.s366-g120: برانگیزد is a present (narrative) verb 'he arouses/spurs/rouses'. اسفندیاری qualifies the following noun (no ezāfe in this poetic line) and functions possessively/adjectivally: 'Esfandiyār's'. سمند is the direct object 'steed/war-horse'. The clause therefore means 'he roused Esfandiyār's steed.'
segments.s366-g201: به روی اندر افتاد is a prepositional phrase + verb: به (a directional preposition 'towards/onto') + روی ('face') + اندر (an older prepositional/adverbial marker 'in/on'), followed by افتاد, the simple past of افتادن 'fell'. The coordinate و links the next predicate بیهوش گشت, where بیهوش is an adjective 'unconscious' and گشت is the past form of گشتن used here as 'became'. Word order is slightly inverted in poetic Persian: prepositional phrase + verb, then a linked predicate introduced by و.
segments.s366-g202: نگفتش is a past negated verb form (ن + گفت + -ش object suffix) meaning 'he did not say (to him / to it)' and takes سخن 'a word / speech' as its object. نیز 'also' or 'either' emphasizes that speaking ceased as well, and the final و خاموش گشت uses و to link the clause to the state 'became silent' where خاموش is the adjective and گشت is 'became'. The order Verb+Object (نگفتش سخن) is idiomatic/poetic.
segments.s366-g203: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; with هوش ('awareness, consciousness') preceded by با (with) the phrase با هوش آمد means 'came to consciousness / regained awareness'. آمد is the past tense 'came'. جهان شهریار ('king of the world') is the subject/title that follows the verb in this poetic word order (verb before subject).
segments.s366-g204: فرود آمد is a compound verbal idea 'came down / descended' (فرود + آمد), followed by the source phrase از تخت 'from the throne'. The coordinating و links the next verb بگریست 'he wept' (past tense), and the adverbial/qualifying زار follows the verb to indicate the manner 'woefully / in lamentation'.
segments.s366-g205: چه باید introduces a rhetorical question 'what should / can (be) ...'. مرا (here shown as a token 'م') is the object 'to me', and the phrase باید ... گفت is a passive/rhetorical construction meaning 'what ought to be said (to me)' or 'what can be said to me'. شاهی here functions as a vocative or address 'O king' / reference to kingship. Poetic order and omission/condensation are typical: the question is elliptical rather than a fully analytic modern structure.
segments.s366-g206: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that/when'); روزم is 'my day' (روز + possessive -م) and here denotes one's fortunes/time. همی + گشت is the habitual/continuous marker in older Persian (همی = 'kept / was continually') with گشتن 'to turn / become' meaning 'my day keeps turning / changing'. خواهد + سیاه forms a future/result clause 'will become dark/black' (خواهد as future auxiliary, سیاه as predicate adjective).
segments.s366-g207: This is a relative clause: که آنان که 'those who' (the first که introduces the clause, the second که is the relative 'who'). بر من (preposition بر + object من) means 'toward me / by me' in the sense 'dear to me', and گرامی‌ترند is the comparative predicate 'are more honored / dearer' with plural present ending -ند. Poetic word order keeps the relative phrases compact.
segments.s366-g208: گزین here functions as an adjective/noun 'chosen / select' (from گزینیدن), followed by سپاهند which presents them as members/elite of the army/host (سپاه). The copular/ending -ند is included in these forms 'they are the chosen (of the) host', and the coordinating و links the second predicate نامی‌ترند 'they are more famed' (comparative + plural copula).
segments.s366-g185: بیابان (desert) is a direct noun that begins the clause; گذارد is a past-form verb here meaning 'set out/entered' or 'took to' and governs the following prepositional phrase. به اندک سپاه is a prepositional phrase giving accompaniment/instrument ('with a small army'): به is used in the sense 'by/with', اندک qualifies سپاه ('a small/few host'). The subject is omitted (typical in Persian narrative) and understood from context.
segments.s366-g186: شود is a verb meaning 'becomes/is made' (here simple/present/gnomic narrative use); the normal order in prose would be شاه پیروز شود but poetry often places the verb first. شاه پیروز (king victorious) is the predicate nominal, and دشمن تباه ('the enemy ruined') is a parallel clause joined by و. تباه is an adjectival predicate meaning 'ruined/destroyed.'
segments.s366-g187: بدان‌ای is an imperative vocative construction: بدان (imperative 'know/consider this') + ای (vocative particle 'O'). گزیده شه خسروان is a compounded vocative phrase: گزیده 'chosen/elite' modifies شه (archaic/poetic form of 'شاه', 'king'), and خسروان ('kings') is used honorifically — together meaning roughly 'O chosen king of kings.'
segments.s366-g188: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'); من is the pronoun 'I'; هرچ is the indefinite relative 'whatever/whatever thing'; گفتم is the past verb 'I said' (here used as the main verb for 'whatever I said'); نباشد جز آن is an idiomatic negative expression meaning 'there is nothing except that' or 'is none other than that' (نباشد is the negative copular/existential, جز 'except', آن 'that'). The clause asserts that everything the speaker said amounts to no more than what follows.
segments.s366-g189: نباشد is the negative existential/coprular ('let there not be' / 'there is not'); ازین یک سخن = 'of this one saying' (ازین = archaic/poetic 'از این' = 'of this' or 'from this'; یک سخن = 'one saying' or 'this single word'). بیش و کم is an idiomatic pair 'more and less' meaning 'more or less' placed after the noun to say 'no more and no less.' The whole phrase restricts speech to exactly one statement.
segments.s366-g190: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'; زین پس ('from now on') is an adverbial phrase where زین is an archaic form corresponding to 'این' and پس 'after/then' — together 'henceforth'. مکن is the negative imperative 'do not.' روی بر من دژم literally 'a grim/sullen face toward me': روی 'face', بر 'toward/on' (directional), من 'me' and دژم an adjective 'sullen/hostile.' The clause orders the addressee to stop looking at the speaker with a hostile face.
segments.s366-g191: که introduces the explanatory clause 'that'; من is 'I'; آنچ is the relative 'that which/what'; گفتم is 'I said' and نگفتم is its negated counterpart 'I did not say' — the sequence here emphasizes that the speaker did not speak spontaneously. مگر after the verb functions as 'except' and prepares the prepositional phrase that follows. Overall: 'that which I said, I did not say except ...'.
segments.s366-g192: به فرمانت is a prepositional phrase 'by your command/order' (به used as 'by/through', فرمان + possessive suffix -ت 'your'). ای شاه پیروزگر is a vocative: ای 'O', شاه 'king', پیروزگر an epithet 'victorious' or 'triumphant.' The phrase supplies the exception introduced by مگر: the speaker acted only by the king's order.
segments.s366-g233: ز اندوه خوردن is a prepositional phrase where ز (from) + the verbal noun خوردن ('being eaten' or 'to be consumed') forms the idiom 'being consumed by sorrow'. نباشدت is an archaic negative copula/impersonal form meaning 'there was not' / 'it would not be', and سود is the predicate 'profit/benefit' — together: 'There was no benefit from being consumed by sorrow.'
segments.s366-g234: کجا here is rhetorical ('what use / where'), بودنی is a verbal adjectival/gerundial form meaning 'that which would be' or 'what was to be', followed by the past copula بود ('was'). و simply links clauses, شد is a perfective 'happened/was done', and کار بود places the event as a completed affair — i.e. 'what was to be was, and the matter was done.'
segments.s366-g235: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not'. دلت را is the object 'your heart' (دل + second-person suffix ت, with را as object marker). بیشتر modifies the degree ('more/too much'), and زین نژند ('this base/ignoble [trapping]') is the thing of attachment — so: 'Do not let your heart cling too much to this base thing.'
segments.s366-g236: بداد here functions in poetic word order as the past action 'gave' (what was given), with خدای جهان 'the Lord of the world/God' as the agent. کن is an imperative 'make/do', and بسند is 'suffice/be enough' — together: 'Let what the Lord of the world has given suffice' or 'make do with what God has given.'
segments.s366-g237: بدادش here is a past verb phrase 'gave to him' (بداد + enclitic ش = 'gave him'), بسی qualifies quantity/intensity ('many/much' in context), and پند 'advice/admonition' is the object. و links to the next clause; بشنید is the past 'he heard', and شاه placed after the verb is the subject ('the king heard').
segments.s366-g238: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when/as'. خورشید گون is an adjectival phrase 'sun-like' (literally 'like the sun'), گشت is the past 'became/turned (to be)', بر شد is a compound action 'rose/stood up' (بر = up/on, شد = became/happened), and به گاه 'at the time/that moment' gives the temporal point — 'when he became sun-like, he rose at that time.'
segments.s366-g239: نشست from برگاه here functions as a past action 'he departed/left the royal court' (نشست can mean to sit or, in context, to withdraw from a seat/place). و links clauses; بنهاد is a past verb 'he placed/put/instilled' (بنهاد دل = 'he set his heart'), and دل is the object 'heart' — 'he left the court and placed his heart (on something).'
segments.s366-g240: به رزم is a prepositional phrase 'toward/in battle'. جهانجوی is an attributive adjective 'world-seeking/ambitious' modifying the following title/name. شاه is 'king', and چگل here functions as a proper epithet/name (literally a word meaning 'tattler' in the lexicon) identifying the king — so: 'on the war of the ambitious King Chaghl.'
segments.s366-g49: وزان is the fused poetic connector (و + زان) meaning “and/from that,” linking this noun phrase to the previous line. زخم (wound) is the head noun; آن گرزهای گران is a post-nominal determiner + noun + adjective group (‘those heavy maces’) showing a genitive/agentive relation to زخم (i.e. wounds inflicted by those maces). Note Persian poetry often omits the spoken ezāfe vowel, so the relation is understood by adjacency.
segments.s366-g50: چنان is a demonstrative/comparative adverb here (“such/like”), introducing a simile that qualifies the previous wounds. پتک پولاد آهنگران should be read as پتکِ پولادِ آهنگران (‘the steel sledgehammer of the blacksmiths’) with the ezāfe implied; پولاد functions as the material/qualifier of پتک and آهنگران as the possessor. The whole segment is a condensed noun phrase forming the comparison.
segments.s366-g51: به گوش اندر آید is a fixed poetic order meaning ‘comes into the ear’ (i.e. ‘is heard’): به (to/towards) + گوش (ear) + اندر (in/into) + آید (comes). ترنگا ترنگ is an onomatopoeic reduplication placed after the verb; it names/imitates the sound heard and functions adverbially. The word order is emphatic and archaic (اندر and the inverted verb placement are stylistically marked).
segments.s366-g52: هوا پر شده is ‘the air has become full’ (هوا = air/atmosphere; پر = full (of); شده = become). نعره governs the following descriptors or names of cries; بور و خنگ here appear as the particular cries or qualities of the cries (presented tersely and onomatopoetically). The coordinating و simply links the two items. Note the compact poetic syntax that leaves the exact relation (adjective vs. named cry) somewhat elliptical.
segments.s366-g53: شکسته شود is a periphrastic subjunctive/jussive construction: شکسته (past participle ‘broken’) + شود (subjunctive of شدن) = ‘be broken’ or ‘may be broken.’ چرخ گردونها is the subject (literally ‘the wheel(s) of the heavens’ or ‘the celestial wheel’), a doubled cosmic image (چرخ + گردون) common in epic diction. The order places the predicate before the subject for emphasis.
segments.s366-g54: زمین سرخ گردد is parallel in form: زمین (earth) + سرخ (red) + گردد (subjunctive/optative of شدن ‘become’), i.e. ‘let the earth become red.’ از ان خونها is an ablative/prepositional phrase (‘from that blood/those bloods’) introduced by از. The verb گردَد here has the classic poetic optative/irrealis force (‘may/let it become’ or in narrative ‘becomes’ depending on tone).
segments.s366-g55: تو گویی is an idiomatic poetic phrase meaning ‘you would say’ or ‘it seems as if’ (گویی used impersonally). هوا ابر دارد همی is literal ‘the air has clouds still/continually’ (هوا = air; ابر = clouds; دارد = has; همی = continuous habitual marker in older/epic Persian). The sequence uses archaic diction (تو گویی, همی) and places the verb دارد after its noun, which is normal in Persian but here has a slightly heightened poetic rhythm.
segments.s366-g56: وزان = و + زان (‘and/from that’), linking to the clouds just mentioned. ابر الماس should be read as ابرِ الماس (‘cloud of diamonds’ or ‘diamond-clouds’) and بارد همی is an archaic present of باریدن ‘to rain/fall’ (بارد here is a verbal form meaning ‘falls/rains’) with همی again marking continuous action: ‘diamonds keep raining from those clouds.’ This بارد is verbal/poetic and not the common adjective meaning ‘cold.’
segments.s366-g121: به خاقان نهد روی پر خشم و تیز is a compact clause in which نهد (3rd-person singular) functions as the main verb meaning "places/puts/turns" and governs روی as its object: literally "he puts/turns (his) face toward the Khaghan." پر + noun (پر خشم) is an adjectival phrase meaning "full of anger," and تیز is another adjectival quality "keen/sharp." The preposition به introduces the goal (خاقان). This word order (verb before some modifiers) is poetic but entirely grammatical in classical Persian.
segments.s366-g122: تو گویی ندیدست هرگز گریز is a parenthetical/observational clause: تو گویی functions like an idiom "as if one would say" or "it seems," followed by the assertion. ندیدست is a classical perfect/present-result form meaning roughly "has not seen" or "has not been seen; never seen," negated by هرگز ("never"). گریز is the noun "flight/retreat" and is the thing he is said never to have known. The sequence places the evaluative particle before the predication in a poetic register.
segments.s366-g123: چو اندر میان بیند ارجاسپ را is a temporal clause introduced by چو (= "when"). اندر میان means "in the middle/among (them)" and modifies the verb بیند (3rd-person singular present of "see"). ارجاسپ is the direct object and is marked by را. Note the verb precedes the object here (poetic word order) but the را marks the object clearly.
segments.s366-g124: ستایش کند شاه گشتاسپ را is a nominal-predicate clause where ستایش ("praise") is the action and کند is the light verb "does/makes" (here functioning as simple present: "praises"). شاه is the subject (the king) and گشتاسپ را is the direct object (Gushtasp) marked by را. Poetic inversion places the verb phrase before the subject and object for emphasis.
segments.s366-g125: صف دشمنان سر بسر بردرد has صف دشمنان = "the line/formation of the enemies." سر بسر is an adverbial repetition meaning "head by head" or "one head after another." بردرد here expresses the action done to that line (poetic verb form meaning to strike/rend/pass through — rendered idiomatically above as "rends"). The clause is compressed and uses fixed epic word-order for forceful effect.
segments.s366-g126: ز گیتی سوی هیچ‌کس ننگرد is a negative statement: ز (poetic from/of) + گیتی (the world) gives the domain, سوی means "toward" and هیچ‌کس is "no one." ننگرد is a present/subjunctive form of نگر (to look/consider) with negative force here: "he does not look toward / he regards no one in the world." The particle order places the domain before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s366-g127: همی خواند او زند زردشت را uses همی to indicate continuous or habitual action ("kept/continually"). خواند is the verbal root meaning "reads/recites" (3rd-person), او is the subject "he," and زند زردشت را (Zand of Zoroaster) is the object marked by را. The order places the adverbial particle first for narrative continuity.
segments.s366-g128: به یزدان نهاده کیی پشت را is a participial/secondary clause: نهاده is a past participle "having placed/entrusted" and takes به یزدان "to/into God" as its complement. کیی modifies پشت ("Kayanian support/backing"), and را marks پشت as the object of نهاده. Together the phrase means "having placed (the) Kayanian support in God" or "having entrusted the Kayanian backing to God."
segments.s366-g17: ببایدت is the classical impersonal/obligative form 'it is necessary for you' with the enclitic ‑ت marking the second person; followed by the infinitive/light verb کردن which here functions as a support verb attaching to the noun شمار. ز اختر is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the stars' that modifies شمار ('counting/ reckoning'). Word order (verb phrase then prepositional modifier then noun) is acceptable in Ferdowsi's verse.
segments.s366-g18: بگویی is a 2nd‑person present/subjunctive/optative verbal form ('you tell' / 'you would tell') used here in a hortatory/reporting sense; همی is the classical continuous/habitual particle ('still/continually') that commonly appears near the verb; مر (archaic) is the object pronoun 'me' placed before the clitic object marker را for emphasis, and را marks the definite object; روی کار is a frozen collocation meaning 'position/standing in affairs' with روی 'face/position' qualifying کار 'affair/office'. The order places the verbal phrase before its object (typical in poetry) and uses duplication/placement of pronoun for metrical/emphatic effect.
segments.s366-g19: که is a subordinating/relative particle 'that/which' introducing an indirect question clause; چون here means 'how' (introducing manner or state) and باشد is the classical 3rd‑person subjunctive/potential 'will be' or 'may be'. آغاز و فرجام جنگ is a coordinated noun phrase 'the beginning and the outcome/end of the battle' serving as the subject/complement of باشد.
segments.s366-g20: کرا is an archaic interrogative/relative pronoun 'which (of them)' or 'who/which', here asking 'which [part] is greater'; بیشتر is the comparative 'more/greater'; باشد again is subjunctive/potential 'will be'; اینجا درنگ is an admonition 'pause here' (اینجا 'here' + درنگ 'pause'), placed at the end for a stage direction or a metrical break.
segments.s366-g21: نیامد here is used idiomatically 'it did not please / it was not pleasing' (rather than literal 'did not come'); خوش is the predicate adjective 'pleasant'; آن پیر جاماسپ را is the topicalized/marked object/experiencer 'that venerable Jamasp' with آن 'that' and پیر as an honorific/epithet for the sage. The object-marking را follows the name, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s366-g22: به روی دژم is an ablative/expressive phrase 'with a grim/sullen face' (به + روی giving manner), where دژم is the adjective 'sullen/grim'; گفت is the verbal root 'said/spoke' (here used as a past tense verb) and is followed by the marked indirect object/topic گشتاسپ را 'to Gushtasp' with را. The order 'manner + verb + indirect object' is typical in classical verse.
segments.s366-g23: که introduces an object/nominal clause explaining the speaker's desire; می‌خواستم is the imperfect/earlier past 'I wished/was wishing'; کایزد is a vocative/exclamation 'O God!' and دادگر is an epithet 'just' (here modifying the vocative, 'O just God'). The sequence expresses a wish/prayer addressed to God.
segments.s366-g24: ندادی is the simple past negative 'you did not give'; مرا (appearing here in the manuscript split/contracted as م ... را) is the object pronoun 'me' combined with the object marker را (here represented in the segment words as separate elements), and این خرد وین هنر is a coordinated noun phrase 'this wisdom and this skill/virtue' (این/وین both demonstratives used for emphasis). The overall structure states that what he had wished for was not granted to him.
segments.s366-g225: گر is the conditional particle 'if' starting the clause. ایشان is the third-person plural pronoun 'they'. نباشند is the negative of بودن in the subjunctive/present third-person plural form here meaning 'are not' / 'were not (present)'; in classical Persian this form is used in hypothetical conditions. پیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of the army' (پیش = 'before/in front of', سپاه = 'army').
segments.s366-g226: This is a compact, inverted poetic clause: نهاده is a past participle 'placed/put'. The phrase is inverted from the neutral order (کلاهِ کیانی را بر سر نهاده): 'the Kayanian cap placed on the head.' Here بسر (poetic contraction of به‌سر / بر سر) + بر function together to mark location 'on the head', and کیانی کلاه is the noun phrase 'Kayanian cap/crown' (possessive/attributive order reversed by poetry).
segments.s366-g227: که here introduces a subordinate/relative clause 'that/who'. یارد شدن is a verbal phrase meaning 'to receive aid / to become aided' (یارد = aid/helper, شدن = 'become' — passive/receival sense). پیش ترکان چین places that action 'before/in the presence of the Turks of China' (پیش = 'before/in front of', ترکان = 'the Turks', چین = 'China' — i.e. 'the Chinese Turks' or 'the Turks of China').
segments.s366-g228: که again introduces a further clause 'that/who'. بازآورد is a verb 'brought back / restored'. فره here is the important noun 'farr' meaning 'divine glory/splendour', پاک is the adjective 'pure', and دین is the governed noun 'religion' — together 'restored the pure divine glory of religion.' Word order is object (فره پاکِ دین) after the verb, a normal poetic order.
segments.s366-g229: This is an imperative address. تو 'you' is the vocative/subject. زین خاک is a poetic contracted locative 'from this dust/earth' (زین = 'from this' in context; خاک = 'earth/dust'). برخیز is the imperative 'rise/stand up'. و is the coordinating 'and'. برشو is another imperative/encouragement 'rise up / get moving / be about' used here in series with برخیز. به گاه is a temporal phrase 'at the proper time / when the time comes' (به = 'at/to', گاه = 'time').
segments.s366-g230: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not (do)'. The phrase فره پادشاهی is a genitival/attributive NP 'the royal/kingsly farr (divine glory of kingship)'. تباه is a predicate adjective 'ruined/destroyed' — together the clause means 'do not let the royal glory be ruined' (literally: 'do not make the kingship's farr ruined').
segments.s366-g231: که introduces an expository clause 'for / that'. داد here means 'justice' (abstract noun). خدایست is a fused form 'is God' or 'belongs to God' (خدا + است), so the clause says 'justice is/belongs to God.' وزین is a compact/conjunctive phrase here (و زین = 'and from this / and in this matter') used classically to link to the next point; چاره نیست is 'there is no remedy/solution' (چاره = remedy, نیست = does not exist). Overall: 'that justice is God's, and for this there is no remedy.'
segments.s366-g232: This is a simple nominal sentence: خداوند means 'the Lord' or 'God', گیتی is 'the world', and ستمگاره نیست literally 'is not an oppressor' (ستمگاره = oppressor/place of oppression, نیست = is not). Word order is subject — adjunct — predicate, yielding 'the Lord of the world is not an oppressor.'
segments.s366-g33: که introduces this subordinate clause ('that' / 'that is, ...'). هرگزت is the adverb هرگز plus the enclitic -ت ('to you'), meaning 'never to you.' روی دشمن کردن is an idiom here: روی (face) + دشمن (enemy) + کردن (کنم) yields 'to turn hostile' or 'become an enemy (to you)'; کنم is first-person present/subjunctive used as a promise/statement of intent.
segments.s366-g34: نفرمایمت is the verb فرمودن with the negative prefix ن- and the 2nd‑person enclitic -مت, meaning 'I will not order/tell you.' بد is the object/adverb 'evil/wrong.' نه ... کنم links two negatives: 'nor will I do (it) myself,' with خود من ('I myself') for emphasis and کنم as the first‑person verb 'do.'
segments.s366-g35: تو is a vocative/emphatic 'you.' هرچ ('whatever') modifies the following noun phrase اندرین کار ('in this affair'); دانی is 2nd‑person present 'you know.' بگوی here is an exhortation 'say/tell' (imperative/jussive) requesting that the addressee speak what they know.
segments.s366-g36: که introduces the explanatory clause 'for/since.' تو چاره‌دانی is a nominal/predicative use meaning 'you are one who knows the remedy/solution' (چاره‌دانی = knowledge/resourcefulness). و connects the clauses and من چاره‌جوی sets up the speaker's role as 'seeker of remedy' (چاره‌جوی = one who seeks a cure/counsel).
segments.s366-g37: خردمند is the subject 'the wise man.' گفت is the verb 'said' in the past tense, introducing direct speech. ای is the vocative particle 'O,' followed by the epithet گرانمایه ('esteemed/precious') and the addressee شاه ('king').
segments.s366-g38: همیشه 'always' is the adverbial modifier. بتو ('to you') marks the beneficiary. تازه is an adjective 'fresh/new.' بادا is an optative/formal prayer particle meaning 'may it be.' کلاه ('hat/crown') is the subject of the optative and appears at the sentence end as the thing wished to remain fresh.
segments.s366-g39: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from' (here marking the target: 'do not [harm] from your servant' = 'do not hurt your servant'). بنده 'servant' is the object. میازار is a negative imperative ('do not harm/trouble'). و 'and' links the two commands; بنداز is the imperative 'throw away/cast off,' with خشم 'anger' as its object.
segments.s366-g40: خنک is a predicative adjective 'pleasant/cool.' آنکسی ('that one') is the demonstrative/indefinite; کو is the archaic relative 'who' (used here as 'whom'), and نبیند is the present negative/optative form of 'see' meaning 'is not seen.' به چشم literally 'by the eye' (= 'to the eye' or 'in sight'). The whole clause states a general observation about someone not seen by the eye.
segments.s366-g169: بیاید is a 3rd-person verbal form used here as a narrative present (‘comes/appears’). پس is a temporal adverb meaning ‘then’ following the verb for poetic word order. آن فرخ اسفندیار is a nominal phrase ('that/ the fortunate Esfandiyar') with آن as the demonstrative and فرخ as an epithet 'fortunate'.
segments.s366-g170: سپاه begins a prepositional phrase giving accompaniment/support: 'an army'. از پس پشت is a stacked prepositional expression meaning 'from/at the rear' (از = 'from', پس = 'after/behind', پشت = 'rear/behind'). و links the following phrase; یزدانش یار reads as 'Yezdan (God) his ally' with the possessive -ش on یزدان indicating 'his God/ divine support' and یار 'companion/ally'.
segments.s366-g171: ابر is the noun 'cloud' and is the direct object of افگند ('threw/raised'), with the postposed adjective بیدرفش ('without a banner', i.e. 'bannerless') describing it. افگند is a past/simple narrative verb 'cast/raised'. اسپ تیز is juxtaposed (appositive or second object) meaning 'a swift steed' (اسپ = horse, تیز = swift).
segments.s366-g172: برو here functions poetically as a locative/attaching element (an older contraction of بر او / 'on him' or similar usage) introducing the garments 'on him' rather than the modern verb 'go' — this is a fixed poetic idiom. جامه 'garment' is described by پر خون 'full of blood' (پر = 'full (of)'), and و دل پر ستیز adds 'and a heart full of strife' (دل = 'heart', ستیز = 'strife').
segments.s366-g173: This clause gives the blow: یکی تیغ هندی 'a certain/one Indian sword' is the subject/agent, زند here is the poetic/historical form of زدن 'struck' (3rd sg). او را marks the person struck as the definite object. مر is an archaic element (here not matching the simple senses listed) and functions as an old dative/particle in the line's word order.
segments.s366-g174: ز بر is a poetic prepositional combination roughly 'from/by (his) side' (ز = 'from', بر = 'on/upon' here forming a locative relation). نیمه تنش 'half his body' is the thing affected (نیمه = 'half', تن = 'body', ش = 'his'). زیر افگند ('threw down under') combines زیر 'under/beneath' with افگند 'threw/cast' to say that half his body was cast down beneath (i.e. toppled/laid low).
segments.s366-g175: بگیرد is a 3rd-person verb (here used as a narrative/instantaneous action 'seized/took hold of'). پس is 'then', and آن آهنین گرز را is the definite object phrase 'that iron mace' (آن = 'that', آهنین = 'iron/steely', گرز = 'mace/club', را marks the definite object).
segments.s366-g176: بتاباند is a causative/past action 'caused to spin / set spinning / swung' (here best taken as 'swung/whirled'). آن فره و برز را is the object affected: آن فره 'that divine radiance/glory' and برز 'exaltation/height' with را marking the object; the verb indicates these were thrown into motion by the blow.
segments.s366-g9: چنان is an adverb meaning 'so' that modifies the following predicate. پاک تن is a noun phrase with the adjective پاک preceding the noun تن (poetic/older word order: adjective before noun) meaning 'a pure body'. بود is the past-tense copula 'was' applying to both coordinated predicates joined by و; تابنده جان is the second coordinated noun phrase (adjective تابنده before noun جان) meaning 'a shining/radiant soul.'
segments.s366-g10: که introduces a result/consequence clause ('so that' / 'such that'). بودی is the past form 'was' (poetic/past usage) that predicates the clause. بر او is a prepositional phrase 'upon/with regard to him' linking the following descriptor to that person. آشکارا نهان is a deliberate poetic oxymoron: آشکارا ('manifestly, openly') modifies نهان ('hidden'), i.e. 'the hidden was manifest.'
segments.s366-g11: ستاره‌شناس is a compound noun 'astronomer/astrologer.' و is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' گرانمایه is an adjective/noun meaning 'esteemed / of high worth.' بود is the past copula 'was.' The whole segment is a simple nominal predicate: 'He was an astronomer and esteemed.'
segments.s366-g12: ابا here is a verbal/lexical item meaning 'to be reluctant / to refuse; to withhold' and takes او ('him') as its object (ابا او = 'he withheld from him' / 'was reluctant toward him'). به دانش specifies the domain ('in matters of knowledge'). کرا is an archaic relative/pronoun 'who/whom' that introduces the relative clause whose predicate is پایه بود ('was a foundation' / 'had a foundation'). Poetic word order places the relative element after the domain phrase.
segments.s366-g13: بپرسید is a past tense verb 'he asked' (third-person past). ازو is the prepositional object 'from him / of him' showing whom the king questioned. شاه is the subject 'the king' (inverted word order: verb before subject). و گفتا links to the reported speech: گفتا is the past 'said.' خدای here begins the reported clause and is the agent/subject in that clause ('God').
segments.s366-g14: ترا is the object 'you' (here written as the clitic ت + object marker را). دین is 'religion' and is one of the direct objects of the verb داد. به links the following noun (داد here is the verb 'gave' rather than the noun 'justice'); داد in this clause is the past verbal 'gave' and takes ترا and دین as its objects. و پاکیزه رای completes the list of things given: پاکیزه ('pure') modifies رای ('opinion/counsel'), so the clause reads literally 'God gave you religion and just/pure counsel.'
segments.s366-g15: چو here functions as 'since / because / as' introducing a concessive/circumstantial clause. تو is the pronoun 'you.' نیست is the negative existential 'is not / there is not.' اندر جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world.' هیچ‌کس is the indefinite 'anyone / no one' completing the thought: 'As there is no one in the world like you.'
segments.s366-g16: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign / world-ruler.' دانش ترا (the enclitic ت + را function as تـ + را = ترا) is the object phrase 'knowledge to you.' داد is the past verb 'gave.' و بس is a closing formula 'and enough / and that is sufficient' (used to conclude or emphasize sufficiency). The clause therefore states that the ruler gave you knowledge, and that suffices.
segments.s366-g209: همی is a preverbal particle marking ongoing or habitual action ('kept/continues to'); رفت is a past tense verb ('he went/kept going'). و links clauses. خواهند is the 3rd‑person plural auxiliary meaning 'they will' (future); the main verb that follows is omitted here and supplied by the next segment. از پیش من is a prepositional phrase literally 'from before me / from my presence' with پیش used as 'in the presence of'.
segments.s366-g210: ز is the poetic/archaic form of 'از' meaning 'from'. تن 'body' is the source from which something is taken. برکنند is a 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive form of برکندن (here used with خواهند to express future 'they will tear off/remove'). اين دل ريش من is the object phrase: اين 'this', دل 'heart', ريش 'beard' (literal 'beard' used here in a metaphorical/poetic sense) and من as the possessive 'my' (here written as a separate word). Word order is object after the verb phrase, as often in Ferdowsi's style the object is explicit with poetic emphasis.
segments.s366-g211: به جاماسپ گفت is 'he said to Jamasp' (به + name marks the indirect object; گفت is the past verb 'said'). ار is the archaic conditional 'if' (modern Persian اگر). چنینست is a fused form meaning 'it is thus' or 'such is'; کار at the end ('the matter/affair') names the subject/topic. The clause order is typical older Persian: indirect object + verb, then conditional phrase.
segments.s366-g212: به هنگام + verbal noun (رفتن) is a temporal expression 'at the time of going'. سوی means 'toward' or 'in the direction of'. کارزار is the battlefield/battle. The whole segment is an adverbial time phrase specifying when the (hypothetical) action would occur.
segments.s366-g213: نخوانم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive with negative prefix 'ن-' meaning 'I will not say/call/summon' (in context 'I will not declare/say'). نبرده is a past participial clause 'has not taken' used here as the content of what would be said; together نخوانم نبرده برادرم را = 'I will not say "they have not taken my brother"'. برادرم is 'my brother' with را as the definite/object marker appended to the clause.
segments.s366-g214: نسوزم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive negative of سوختن used figuratively 'I will not let (it) burn / I will not grieve'. دل پیر مادرم را is the object: دل 'heart', پیر 'old', مادرم 'my mother' (here shown with a separate possessive element), and را marks it as the object affected by the verb. The clause means 'I will not allow my aged mother's heart to burn' (i.e. to be consumed by sorrow).
segments.s366-g215: نفرمایمش is composed of نفرمایم ('I will not command/say') plus the object clitic -ش ('him'), so 'I will not command/order him'. نیز means 'also'. رفتن به رزم is a verbal noun phrase 'to go to battle'. Together the segment says 'I will not also command him to go to battle.'
segments.s366-g216: سپه را is the direct object 'the army' marked by را. سپارم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive (here future/intentional) of سپردن 'to entrust/assign' — 'I will entrust'. به introduces the beneficiary; فرخ گرزم is a personal name (compound) to whom the army is entrusted. Word order is object + verb + prepositional beneficiary, a common pattern in older Persian.
segments.s366-g137: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' (not a simile). The clause follows with شاه جهان (king of the world) as subject and بازگردد (return) as a 3rd-person verb; بازگردد is a present/subjunctive form used narratively to mean 'returns' or 'comes back'. ز رزم is an archaic prepositional phrase (ز = 'from') giving the source 'from battle'.
segments.s366-g138: گرفته and کشته are past-participle/adjectival forms used elliptically to describe results: 'having taken' and 'having killed'. جهان را is the definite object marked by را for گرفته. گرزم is گرز + ـم (the instrument 'mace' with the first‑person possessive suffix -م = 'my mace'), so the phrase implies killing with 'my mace' (poetic/elliptical expression of the instrument).
segments.s366-g139: بیندازد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive narrative verb 'casts/lets fly'. آن ترک is the topicalized 'that Turk'. تیری is the object 'an arrow'. بروی here is archaic/fused (بر + روی) meaning 'upon the face / at the face' (an adverbial of direction); the orthography shows a fused form common in poetry.
segments.s366-g140: This short clause is lexically difficult/corrupt in the transmitted reading. شدن functions as a verbal auxiliary ('become' / 'happen'); آشکارا is an adverb 'openly, plainly'. The overall sense intended is probably 'it does not (or does) become apparent/meet him openly on the face', but the exact form نیارد is uncertain in the manuscript tradition; I mark that word as uncertain and read the clause as describing that the arrow does not openly strike him.
segments.s366-g141: پس از دست = 'then/by the hand of' (پس = then; از دست = 'from the hand of', idiomatically 'by the hand of'). آن modifies the following noun. بیدرفش is a compound adjective 'without a banner / pennonless' and پلید is an adjectival epithet 'vile/base', both qualifying 'that (man)'.
segments.s366-g142: شود is the present/subjunctive of شدن used here in a passive/result sense 'is made / becomes'. شاه آزادگان 'the king of the free' (آزادگان = 'the free, the noble') is the patient/affected NP, and ناپدید is the predicate adjective 'vanished / gone' — together meaning 'is made to vanish' (i.e. is killed or disappears).
segments.s366-g143: به ترکان 'to the Turks' (به + plural noun). برد باره is a light verbal phrase: برد (take/carry) + باره (load/booty) — together 'carried the spoil/booty'. و is the conjunction 'and'. زین اوی = زین + اوی ('saddle' + possessive 'his') — 'his saddle'. The segment depicts taking spoils and the saddle to the Turks.
segments.s366-g144: بخواهد here is a future/optative auxiliary 'will wish/seek'. The verb precedes the subject in inverted poetic order: بخواهد پسرت = 'his son will seek'. آن زمان 'at that time'. کین اوی = 'revenge for him' (کین 'vengeance'; اوی is the third‑person possessive/pronoun 'his/for him'). The whole means 'his son then wished/desired his revenge'.
segments.s366-g161: This is a verb-first poetic clause: شوند/شود (here third-person singular) functions as the main verb 'become/are (made)', followed by the noun phrase describing what happens. 'کشته' is a past participle/noun 'the slain'; 'چندان' quantifies it as 'so many/much'. 'ز هر سو سپاه' is a prepositional/partitive phrase 'from every side of the host' (ز = 'from', هر سو = 'every side', سپاه = 'host/army'). The overall order (verb before subject phrase) is common in Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s366-g162: 'که' introduces a result clause 'that...'. 'از خونشان' = 'from/of their blood' (از = 'from/out of', خون = 'blood', شان = 'their'). 'پر شود' is a compound predicate: 'پر' = 'full (of)' and 'شود' = 'becomes' so 'becomes full'. 'رزمگاه' is the noun being described 'the battlefield'. The clause thus gives the consequence: the battlefield becomes full of their blood.
segments.s366-g163: 'پس' is a discourse adverb 'then'. 'آن' is the demonstrative 'that', introducing the person. 'بیدرفش' is a compound epithet 'without banner/flagless'. 'پلید و سترگ' are coordinated adjectives: 'پلید' = 'vile/filthy' and 'سترگ' = 'with a large gaze/sight' (an older word for 'gaze' or 'wide-eyed'). The phrase is a nominal group describing the warrior before the verb.
segments.s366-g164: 'به پیش اندر آید' is a verbal phrase meaning 'comes forward/advances': 'به' + 'پیش' = 'to the front', and the verb 'آید' = 'comes'. 'اندر' is an older preposition meaning 'into/in' used here for motion toward the front. 'چو' introduces a simile 'like', followed by 'ارغنده گرگ' (a proper name 'Argandeh' with 'گرگ' 'the wolf') comparing his advance to that wolf.
segments.s366-g165: 'همان' = 'that same' emphasizes the referent; 'تیغ' = 'sword'. 'زهر آب‌داده' is a compound participial phrase literally 'having been given (moistened) with poison' — i.e. 'drenched/steeped in poison'. 'به دست' is an instrumental/locative phrase 'in (his) hand' (به + دست), describing the sword's position.
segments.s366-g166: 'همی' is a verbal particle meaning continued or habitual action ('keeps on', 'continually'). 'تازد' (archaic perfective/epic form of تاختن) = 'rushes/attacks'. 'او' = 'he'. 'باره' here functions adverbially 'again/another time'. 'چون پیل مست' is a simile 'like a drunken elephant' (پیل = 'elephant', مست = 'drunk/intoxicated'), describing his charging style. The clause overall conveys repeated, furious charges.
segments.s366-g167: 'به دست وی' is an instrumental phrase 'by his hand' (به used instrumentally, وی = 'him'); 'اندر فراوان سپاه' places the action 'within/amid the numerous army' (اندر = 'in/among', فراوان = 'numerous/abundant', سپاه = 'host/army'). This whole prepositional cluster sets agent and setting.
segments.s366-g168: 'تبه گردد' is a predicative phrase where 'تبه' (here noun 'ruin/mischief') combines with 'گردد' (becomes/happens) to mean 'ruin comes' or 'is brought about'. 'از برگزینان شاه' identifies the affected group: 'از' = 'of/from' and 'برگزینان شاه' = 'the king's chosen (men)'. The clause says the king's chosen suffer ruin as a result.
segments.s366-g177: به یک حمله is a prepositional phrase 'with/by one assault' (به + numeral phrase). از جایشان بگسلد: جایشان ('their places') follows the preposition از, and بگسلد is a third-person, perfective/archaic narrative verb 'he tore/broke (them) away' (ب‑ prefix is characteristic of older/poetic forms). The clause reads literally 'with one assault he tore them from their places.'
segments.s366-g178: چو is the temporal 'when'. بگسستشان is the verb phrase 'broke them' (past/perfective, object clitic -شان). بر زمین = 'to/on the ground'. کی is an interrogative 'who'. هلد is an archaic/obscure item here; its exact sense is not provided in the supplied lexicon, but the phrase functions as an interrogative 'who could (then)...?'.
segments.s366-g179: بنوک سر نیزه‌شان is a genitive phrase 'to/the tip (بنـوک) of the spear-head (سر نیزه‌شان)'. بر چند here is an interrogative/elliptical marker ('how many' / 'in how many [places/times]') that links forward to the verb in the next segment; the phrase as a whole specifies location/part (the spear-tips) that the action affects.
segments.s366-g180: کندشان is the verb کند (to do/make) with the object clitic -شان 'them' → 'he made them ...' or 'he did to them ...'. تبه پاک is an adverbial/qualifying phrase: تبه here = ruin/ruinous, پاک = 'completely/utterly' (poetic use of پاک as intensifier). و بپراگند is a coordinated verb phrase: بپراگند is a past/poetic form 'and he scattered them' (ب‑ prefix + root پراگَندن). Overall: 'he made them utterly ruined and scattered them.'
segments.s366-g181: گریزد is an archaic/narrative third-person verb form meaning 'he fled' (perfective/past in epic usage). سرانجام is the adverb 'at last/finally' placed before the agent phrase سالار چین ('the commander/lord of China'), so the clause reads 'at last the commander of China fled.'
segments.s366-g182: از اسفندیار is the prepositional phrase 'from Esfandiyar'. آن گو بافرین is an appositive epithet attached to the name: آن 'that/the', گو here functions as an archaic adjectival element, and بافرین is an epithet 'praiseworthy / glorious'. Together they mean 'that glorious one' referring to Esfandiyar.
segments.s366-g183: به ترکان is 'toward the Turks' (به = toward/to). نهد is the verb 'he set/turned' (poetic past form of نهاد used as 'placed/turned'). روی 'face' is the object and بگریخته is a participle/adjective 'fleeing/put to flight' modifying روی: literally 'he turned his fleeing face toward the Turks' — i.e., he faced the Turks while in flight or turned toward the Turks as they fled.
segments.s366-g184: This is a verbless/nominal clause summarizing the battlefield state: شکسته (past participle) 'broken' modifies سپر 'shields', and نیزها (plural) 'spears' is followed by ریخته, a perfective participial/verb form 'strewn/laid/fallen'. Together: 'shields broken, spears scattered.' Note: the provided senses list for نیزها did not contain the expected sense 'spear (plural)', so that item is marked unknown below.
segments.s366-g73: بسی is a poetic/archaic quantifier placed before the noun phrase meaning 'many' (the provided lemma senses don't include this exact meaning, so it functions as an intensive). نامداران is a plural noun 'renowned men' and گردان چین is a compound noun phrase meaning 'the troop(s) of Chin' (گردان = troop, چین = Chin/China); و simply coordinates the two noun phrases.
segments.s366-g74: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that' modifying the preceding noun phrase. آن is a demonstrative 'that/the' modifying شیرِ مرد, a compound epithet 'lion-man' (شیر used metaphorically for a valiant warrior, مرد = man/hero). افگند is the archaic past form of افکندن 'to throw/cast' (3sg past) and بر زمین is the prepositional phrase 'onto the ground' that follows the verb.
segments.s366-g75: سرانجام is an adverb 'in the end/at last.' بختش is بخت 'fortune' with the enclitic -ش 'his' (his fortune) and کند is the common present/preterital verb 'does/makes' (3sg). خاکسار here is a predicate noun meaning 'humble/abased one' or 'humble supplicant' — together the clause means 'in the end his fortune makes him humble/abase him.'
segments.s366-g76: برهنه کند is 'makes (it) bare' (برهنه = bare/uncovered, کند = makes/does). آن سر تاجدار is the direct object: آن 'that', سر 'head', تاجدار 'crowned' (together 'that crowned head'). Word order is verb then object, typical of classical Persian narrative lines.
segments.s366-g77: بیاید here is an optative/volitive form meaning 'may (he) come' or 'let (him) come.' پس آنگاه are temporal adverbs 'then/afterwards' (stacked for emphasis). فرزند من is the nominal phrase 'my son' (فرزند = child/son, من as the possessor). The overall clause is a wish: 'May then my son come.'
segments.s366-g78: ببسته is a past/perfect verbal form (has fastened/has tied). میان را is the definite object 'the middle/the breast' marked by را. جگر بند من is the subject/agent phrase in apposition: جگر 'the heart' (figurative seat of feeling) + بند 'binder/bond' forming a compound epithet 'heart-binder' and من 'my' as possessor; literal order is Verb – Object – Subject (V O S), so 'my heart-binder has fastened my middle/breast.'
segments.s366-g79: This is a stacked epithet + name + apposition: ابر کین is a metaphorical epithet 'the vengeful/anger-cloud' modifying the proper name شیدسپ (Shidasp). فرزند شاه is an appositive 'son of the king.' Classical Persian often juxtaposes such modifiers without an izāfa link.
segments.s366-g80: به میدان کند literally 'puts/turns to the field' i.e. 'rides into the field/charges into battle' (به = into/towards + میدان = field/arena; کند = makes/does, used here with the motion sense). تیز اسپ سیاه is the noun phrase 'swift black steed' where تیز 'swift' and سیاه 'black' modify اسپ 'steed/horse' (classical order places adjectives around the noun).
segments.s366-g65: ولیکن is a conjunctive adverb meaning 'but/yet' introducing a contrast. سرانجام is an adverb 'in the end/finally'. کشته شود is a passive verbal phrase: کشته is the past participle 'killed/slain' and شود is the classical auxiliary (subjunctive/optative form of شدن) used here to form a passive predicate 'will be/is killed'; note the verb-final order typical of Persian.
segments.s366-g66: نکونامش is نکونام + enclitic -ش ('his'), literally 'his good name' or 'his famed name'. اندر is the old-fashioned preposition 'in/within'. نوشته شود is again a passive verbal phrase: نوشته (written) + شود (auxiliary), meaning 'will be written' or 'is recorded'; object phrase precedes the verb as usual in Persian.
segments.s366-g67: دریغ is an interjection/exclamation 'pity/alas' expressing regret. آنچنان is an intensifier 'such/so', and مرد نام‌آورا is a nominal phrase 'a man famous (for his name)' — here مِرد (hero/man) + نام (reputation) + آورا (forming the idea 'name-bearing' = 'famed'); the adjective-like element follows the noun in this poetic order.
segments.s366-g68: ابا here is a verb meaning 'to refuse/abstain from'; in this poetic line the verb appears before the subject (inverted order). رادمردان همه = 'all the noblemen' (رادمردان 'noble/virtuous men' modified by همه 'all'), and سرورا is the object 'delights/pleasures' which they 'refused'—so: 'the noblemen all refused (the) pleasures.'
segments.s366-g69: پس is a discourse adverb 'then/thereafter'. آزاده is an honorific/adjective 'noble' or 'free-hearted' placed before the proper name (آزاده شیدسپ = 'the noble Shidasp'). فرزند شاه is a genitive apposition 'son/descendant of the king' following the name.
segments.s366-g70: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. درآید is a third-person form of درآوردن/درآمدن 'to enter/appear' (here 'he appears/comes'), placed after the subject as is normal. به روی سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'before/upon the face of the army' (به + روی meaning 'in front of/against the host'), locating Rostam's arrival.
segments.s366-g71: پس آنگاه is a tautological pair of temporal adverbs 'then/at that moment' emphasizing sequence. مر is an archaic/clitic first‑person object 'me' (appearing before the object), تیغ را is the definite object 'the sword' (را marks definiteness/object), and برکشد is a prefixed verb (بر- + کشد) meaning 'to draw/raise' (here 'raised/drew [the sword]'); verb-final order places the action last.
segments.s366-g72: بتازد is an imperfective/narrative present of تاختن 'to rush/charge/gallop' (he charges). بسی here is an intensifier meaning 'many/much' in classical usage modifying اسپ 'steeds' (beyond the provided senses), so it denotes numerous horses. و is the coordinating 'and', دشمن is 'the enemy', and کشد is the verb form meaning 'he kills/slays' in this narrative present; note that some listed lemma senses do not include 'many' or 'to kill', so those entries are marked accordingly.
segments.s366-g41: بدان‌ای is the imperative 'know this' with the vocative clitic ای attached ('Know this, O...'). نبرده is a negative participle/adjective here ('not having taken' or 'un‑won'), followed by the relative/identifier کی 'who', and نامجوی is a compound/epithet 'name‑seeker' (one who seeks a name = seeker of fame). Together they form the vocative phrase 'O seeker of fame who has not won (it)'.
segments.s366-g42: چو begins a temporal clause 'when'. در رزم is a locative phrase 'into/in the battle'. روی is the noun 'face' (the image of showing one’s face). اندر + آری + بروی here express the action of presenting oneself to battle: اندر آری is an older/poetic circumlocution ('you bring/put into') and بروی is the present/subjunctive/imperative sense 'you go/advance' — so: 'when you bring your face into battle, go forth.'
segments.s366-g43: بدانگه is an adverb 'thereupon/then'. کجا here is an interrogative/adverb 'where' used with the following verbs. بانگ و ویله کنند is a coordinated verbal phrase: بانگ 'cries/shouts' and ویله (an obscure/poetic term here, functioning as a noun 'wail/clamor'), with کنند (present tense plural) 'they make/utter' — 'they utter cries and wails.'
segments.s366-g44: تو گویی همی is an idiomatic, somewhat impersonal turn meaning 'you would say' or 'one would think' (تو acting as a dummy pronoun, گویی from گفتن used impersonally, and همی adding emphasis). کوه را برکنند is a hyperbolic object clause 'they would uproot the mountain' (را marks the definite object, برکنند is plural subjunctive/indicative 'they uproot/remove').
segments.s366-g45: به پیش (lit. 'to/for the front') means 'forward' or 'to the fore'. اندر آیند is a present plural verb 'they come in/advance'. مردان مرد is a doubled/epithetic construction ('men of men') meaning 'the foremost men' or 'true heroes' — a common epic intensifying epithet.
segments.s366-g46: هوا تیره گردد is a nominal clause with گرد(د) 'become' meaning 'the air grows dark/ gloomy'. ز گرد نبرد is an ablative/causal phrase 'from/with the گرد of battle'; here گرد is 'dust' or 'cloud (of dust)' raised by fighting, so 'because of the dust of battle'.
segments.s366-g47: جهان را ببینی is a second‑person future/immediate-perceptual turn 'you will see the world' (ببینی as present/future perception verb). بگشته کبود is a perfective/resultative phrase: بگشته 'has become/been turned' + کبود 'dark/blue‑dusky', so 'the world has grown dusky/dark.'
segments.s366-g48: زمین پر ز آتش is a genitive construction 'the earth full of/fire with fire' (پر ز = 'full of'). هوا پر زدود is parallel: هوا 'the air' + پر (full of) + زدود here functions idiomatically as 'full of smoke' (in the poem the surface string is to be read as ز دود = 'of smoke'); taken together: 'the earth filled with fire, the air filled with smoke.'
segments.s366-g193: وزان is the poetic contraction of و + زان ("and about that"); کم modifies the verb and means "a little/only briefly". بپرسید is past third-person singular "asked"; فرخنده شاه is the subject (an epithet + noun) placed after the verb by the poem's inverted word order, so: "the auspicious king asked (a little) about that."
segments.s366-g194: ازین is از + این ("from/about this") and introduces the complement of بپرسید. ژرف دریا is a compound adjective+noun "deep sea"; و تاریک راه coordinates the second noun phrase "and the dark path." Together this prepositional phrase answers what he asked about.
segments.s366-g195: ندیدم is the past first-person negative "I did not see/foresee." که introduces the subordinate clause. بر + شاه means "from/upon the king" (here: toward or in relation to the king). بنهفتمی is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning roughly "I should hide/withdraw (myself)" or "I would have hidden/kept silence"; together: "I did not see that I ought to hide from the king."
segments.s366-g196: وگرنه is a conditional/adversative "otherwise." من is the first-person pronoun. این راز = "this secret." کی گفتمی is an archaic conditional/quotative clause: کی here functions emphatically ("ever/would ever") and گفتمی is a past/conditional form meaning "I would have told/said" — so the whole clause says "otherwise I would never have told this secret."
segments.s366-g197: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning "when." شاه جهاندار is the compound subject "the king, the world-ruler/sovereign." بشنید is simple past third-person "he heard," and راز is the direct object "the secret."
segments.s366-g198: بران (= بر آن) functions adverbially "thereupon/on that." گوشه تخت is a possessive noun phrase "the corner of the throne." خسپید is a poetic form (here equivalent to خسبید/خوابید) meaning "he lay down/reclined" rather than a literal "becoming white;" باز is an adverb "again/once more." Together: "thereupon he again lay down on the throne's corner."
segments.s366-g199: ز (poetic for از) introduces the source "from his hand." دستش is "his hand" (دست + possessive suffix -ش). بیفتاد is past third-person "fell." زرینه گرز is the noun phrase "the golden mace" and is the thing that fell; the phrase order here (source first, verb, then nominative noun) reflects poetic inversion but yields "the golden mace fell from his hand."
segments.s366-g200: تو گفتی is an address to "you" ("you said" / "as you said"). برفتش is a verbal compound meaning "befell him/it went upon him." همی is a small particle used here as an intensifier/linking particle ("indeed/thereupon"). فر و برز are two paired abstract nouns: فر = "divine/royal glory (farr)" and برز = "exaltation/highness;" linked by و "and" as the things that came upon him.
segments.s366-g89: برین سان is the fused poetic form of بر این سان = "in this manner" (بر + این/ین + سان). همی‌افگند is the habitual/imperfective stating repeated action: همی (habitual marker) + افگند (throw/ cast), so here "he kept hurling" or "he would hurl." دشمنان follows as the definite/indefinite object (no را because of poetic word order) meaning "the enemies."
segments.s366-g90: همی again marks a recurring/habitual action. برکند is the verb (برکندن) meaning "pluck up/tear out" used here in the same habitual/narrative sense "would pluck/tear out." جان آهرمنان is the direct object phrase: جان = "life/soul," modified by the genitive/possessive آهرمنان (the Ahrimans/devils), so "pluck the lives of the Ahrimans."
segments.s366-g91: سرانجام is an adverb meaning "at last/in the end" placed frontally. در جنگ sets the locative "in battle," and کشته شود is a passive construction: کشته (past participle/adjective "killed/slain") + شود (copula/auxiliary) giving "is slain" (narrative/passive sense).
segments.s366-g92: نکو نامش is a noun phrase: نکو (adjective "good, noble") modifies نام + -ش (possessive clitic "his") = "his good name/reputation." اندر + نوشته + شود is a locative + past-participle + auxiliary pattern: اندر نوشته ("in the writing/inscription") with شود as auxiliary = "is written/recorded."
segments.s366-g93: پس is a temporal/conclusive particle "then." ازاده is an epithet used as a noun here meaning "the noble one." بستور in position with پور زریر is the personal name ("Bastur"), followed by پور زریر = "son of Zarir" (پور = "son/descendant," زریر = proper name). The phrase is an appositive/name group: "then the noble Bastur, son of Zarir."
segments.s366-g94: به پیش forms a directional adverbial "forward/ahead." افگند is the past verb "threw/shot" (simple past narrative). اسپ is the object "steed/horse." چون + نره شیر is a simile "like a male lion," where نره modifies شیر to specify a male lion. Word order is adverbial (به پیش) + verb (افگند) + object (اسپ) + simile.
segments.s366-g95: بسی here is a quantifier "many" (classical usage) that modifies دشمنان را (the object marked by را, the definite object marker). کند (present/habitual/poetic form of کردن) + ناپدید yields a causative/result phrase "made (them) disappear" or "caused (them) to vanish." The order is quantifier + object + را + verb + predicate adjective.
segments.s366-g96: شگفتی‌تر is the comparative "more astonishing/more wonderful." از کار او is the comparison phrase "than his deed" (از introduces the compared element, کار = "deed/act," او = "his/that of him"). کس ندید is a negative existential statement: کس (in this line used with negation) + ندید (past negative of دیدن) = "no one saw" or "no one has seen." Together: "No one has seen anything more astonishing than his deed."
segments.s366-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. از بلخ is a prepositional phrase 'from Balkh' modifying the verb; بامی is a noun 'roof/top' here indicating the party/location involved (poetic diction). به جیحون رسید is a past action: رسید (3rd sg. past) 'reached' with به + place 'to/toward the Jihun'.
segments.s366-g2: سپهدار is the subject 'the commander'. لشکر is the noun 'army/host' (direct object). فرود آورید is a compound: فرود (descent) + آورد(َ)ید (here an older past/casualative sense) meaning 'caused to descend' / 'ordered to dismount'; verb-final word order is normal in this epic register.
