segments.s366-g3: بشد is an archaic/past narrative verb meaning 'it happened / he came into view' (here 'appeared' or 'came forth'); شهریار is the subject 'the king'. از میان سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the middle of the army' (از + میان = 'from among').
segments.s366-g4: فرود آمد is a past compound 'alighted / dismounted' (فرود + آمد). از باره 'from the bāra/borne load' here means 'from his litter/baggage' (poetic use of بار/باره). بر شد is a light verbal phrase 'got off / alighted (onto the ground)' and به گاه is a temporal adjunct 'at that time / at the moment'; the line uses flexible, archaic word order typical of Ferdowsi.
segments.s366-g5: بخواند is past of خواندن here meaning 'he summoned/called'. او is the pronoun 'him/he' used along with the explicit noun (a common poetic duplication for emphasis), گرانمایه 'noble/esteemed' modifies جاماسپ, and را marks جاماسپ as the definite object.
segments.s366-g6: کجا is the interrogative adverb 'where'. رهنمون is a noun 'guide' used as predicate; بود is the past copula 'was'. گشتاسپ را is the marked noun 'Gushtasp' with را showing him as the one related to 'guide' (i.e. 'the guide for Gushtasp'). The word order is inverted for emphasis/question.
segments.s366-g7: سر موبدان is a genitive phrase 'head/chief of the mobeds' (سر = 'chief/leader' + موبدان 'priests'). بودو is an archaic contracted form of بود و 'was and', linking to the next title. شاه ردان means 'king/sovereign of the rādān' (here 'rādān' = counselors/advisors in context); titles are listed in appositive fashion.
segments.s366-g8: چراغ is used metaphorically 'lamp / beacon / guide' (i.e. a guiding light). بزرگان و اسپهبدان is a coordinated phrase 'to/for the great ones and the spahbeds (military commanders)', with و linking the two groups; the whole phrase describes whom he illuminated or guided.
segments.s366-g57: بسی is an adverb meaning ‘many/often’ modifying the clause; بی‌پدر گشته is a participial clause (بی‌پدر = ‘fatherless’, گشته = perfect/participial form ‘has become’) and بینی is the present 2nd-person verb ‘you see’, placed before the noun پسر which names the patient. The phrase literally reads ‘you see a son having become fatherless’ (i.e. “you will often see sons left fatherless”).
segments.s366-g58: Parallel structure to the previous segment: بسی again ‘many/often’; بی پسر (‘without a son’) + گشته (‘has become’) forms a participial clause; بینی ‘you see’ is the matrix verb; پدر ‘father’ is the noun being described. Together: ‘you see many a father become without a son’ (i.e. fathers bereft of sons). Note the inversion: the participle precedes the main verb بینی and the head noun (پدر) comes at the end for emphasis.
segments.s366-g59: A nominal phrase: نخستین ‘the first/foremost’ qualifies کس ‘person’ (here ‘one’), followed by the epithet نام‌دار ‘famed’; اردشیر is the proper name in apposition. In other words: ‘the foremost famed one, Ardashir.’
segments.s366-g60: پس is a temporal/continuative adverb ‘then/afterwards’; شهریار is the subject ‘the king’; آن ‘that’ points to the previously named person; نبرده is a perfect/negated verbal form of بردن here ‘did not take/defeat’; دلیر is the object/description ‘the brave one’. The clause therefore says roughly ‘then the king did not defeat that brave (man)’. The verb/object order is compacted in poetry but functions as Subject–(then)–Verb–Object.
segments.s366-g61: به پیش is an adverbial phrase ‘forward/in front’; افگند is the past tense of افکندن ‘threw/placed’; اسپ تازان خویش is a noun phrase ‘his swift steeds’ where تازان (from تاز) describes the horses and خویش is composed of خوی + the pronominal suffix ش = ‘his own’. The whole clause means ‘he cast his swift horses forward.’
segments.s366-g62: به خاک افگند = ‘threw to the ground’ (به + خاک + past verb). هر ک (هر + ک contraction of که) introduces a free relative ‘whoever/whichever’; آیدش is present 3sg ‘comes to him’ with the clitic ش ‘to him’; پیش here means ‘before/in front of (him)’. So: ‘he threw to the ground whoever came before him.’ The present tense here gives a habitual/general sense common in epic narration.
segments.s366-g63: پیاده کند is a causative/habitual phrase ‘(he) makes (them) go on foot’ = ‘unhorses’; کند is the present/habitual form of کردن used here narratively; ترک is the subject ‘Turks’; چنان/چندان modifies سوار to mean ‘so many’ or ‘to such an extent’ and سوار = ‘riders/horsemen’. The clause thus expresses ‘he unhorsed so many Turkish horsemen.’
segments.s366-g64: کز is the relative ‘that/whose/whom’; اختر ‘star’ is used idiomatically (parallel to countless stars); نباشد here functions as negative copula/subjunctive ‘is not / it is not possible’; مر = ‘to me’ (dative clitic in older Persian); آن را شمار = ‘to count that/its number’. Together the line says the number is so great ‘that I cannot count it’ (i.e. ‘so many that I cannot count them’). Word order and the clitic مر are archaic/poetic.
segments.s366-g129: سرانجام is an adverb ‘at last’/‘in the end’. گردد is the 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of گردیدن used here to indicate the resulting state (‘will become / will be’). برو تیره‑بخت functions as the predicate with تیره‑بخت as the subject: برو (literally “go/away”) is used poetically to mean ‘be gone/perish’, describing the fate of the ill‑fated man.
segments.s366-g130: بریده is a past‑participle/adjectival form ‘cut off’. کندش is a cliticized verb (کردن + ـش) meaning ‘does it to him’ or ‘cuts him (off)’; the verb follows the participle in this poetic order. آن نکو is a noun phrase ‘that noble/good one’ (آن determiner + adjective نکو). تاج و تخت are the direct objects ‘crown and throne’ (noun + coordinating و).
segments.s366-g131: بیاید is a poetic present/subjunctive form of آمدن meaning ‘comes / appears’. یکی is an indefinite ‘a certain/someone’. نام او (= ‘his name’) is followed by بیدرفش, here functioning as a proper/name or epithet (‘Bidarfash’ literally ‘without‑banner’); the copula is omitted (typical in Persian equative clauses).
segments.s366-g132: به سرنیزه is a locative phrase ‘on/at the spear‑tip’ with به marking position. دارد is the present tense ‘he has / bears’. درفش بنفش is a noun + adjective ‘a purple standard’ (adjective follows noun in Persian). The whole clause therefore means ‘he carries a purple standard on the spear‑point’.
segments.s366-g133: نیارد is an older/poetic noun meaning ‘companion / ally’; with شدن it forms an intransitive/passive‑like phrase ‘is set / is placed’ (شدن = ‘become / be placed’). پیش گرد گزین is a compound notion ‘the chosen advance‑guard’ (پیش گرد = advance/vanguard, گزین = chosen). The segment thus means roughly ‘a companion is put forward as the chosen vanguard’.
segments.s366-g134: نشیند is the 3rd‑person form of نشستن ‘sit’ used here in the sense ‘lies in wait / takes up position’. به راه وی = ‘on/along his road/way’ (به marking direction/location, وی = him). اندر کمین is an older prepositional phrase ‘in ambush’ (اندر = in, کمین = ambush).
segments.s366-g135: باستد is a past/archaic form of بودن here ‘stood / was standing’ (با‑ + استد used poetically). بران راه = ‘on that road’ (بران = ‘on/thereon’, راه = road). چون ... پیل مست is a simile introduced by چون ‘like’: پیل ‘elephant’ + مست ‘intoxicated / frenzied’ – a figurative comparison to a huge, raging beast.
segments.s366-g136: یکی is ‘a certain / one’. تیغ زهر آب‌داده is a noun phrase where تیغ ‘sword’ is qualified by a participial phrase زهر آب‌داده ‘having been watered/dipped with poison’ (poetic way to say ‘poison‑dipped sword’). به دست is a locative/instrumental phrase ‘in (his) hand’ (به marking the relation, دست = hand).
segments.s366-g145: پس is an adverb meaning 'then' marking sequence. آن + لشکر make the noun phrase 'that army' (demonstrative + noun); نامدار and بزرگ are adjectival modifiers following the head noun (typical Persian order: noun + modifiers in poetry), so لشکر نامدار بزرگ = 'the renowned, great army.'
segments.s366-g146: به دشمن درافتد is a verb phrase with the verb درافتد (present/narrative third-person singular of درافتادن) at the end: 'falls upon/attacks (toward) the enemy' (به + دشمن marks target). چو introduces a simile 'like/as', followed by شیر 'lion'. سترگ here is an epithet 'mighty/large' modifying شیر (poetic adjective after the noun).
segments.s366-g147: همی تازند is an ongoing/habitual present: همی ('continually/still') with تازند (third-person plural) = 'they keep charging/rush forward.' این بر آن آن برین is a terse, reciprocal sequence: این بر آن = 'this upon that' and آن برین is a poetic contraction of آن بر این = 'that upon this' — the repetition and inversion emphasize mutual assault.
segments.s366-g148: ز خون یلان is a prepositional phrase 'from/because of the blood of the heroes' (ز = 'from/because of', خون = 'blood', یلان = 'heroes'). سرخ گردد زمین is a poetic word order: predicate adjective سرخ + verb گردد ('become') + subject زمین = 'the earth becomes red.' The genitive relation ('blood of the heroes') is expressed without an explicit ezāfe in this terse poetic sequence.
segments.s366-g149: یلان را topicalizes 'the heroes' (را marking them as topic/emphasized object). بباشد is an archaic/stative form of 'to be' here (third-person present), giving 'as for the heroes, all (their) faces are/turn ...'. همه روی زرد = 'all (their) faces [become] pale' — روی is 'face' with the possessive understood from the context (their faces).
segments.s366-g150: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when/whenas.' لرزه برافتد is an idiom 'a trembling falls/comes' (برافتد = 'befall/happen'), i.e., 'when a tremor seizes [them]'. به مردان مرد: به + مردان marks the group affected ('upon the men'); مردان مرد is a poetic intensifying apposition ('men who are men' = 'the bravest/valiant men'), with the second مرد functioning as an epithet.
segments.s366-g151: برآید is third-person singular present 'rises/appears.' به خورشید gives the direction 'toward the sun.' گرد سپاه is a genitival phrase 'the dust of the army' (گرد = 'dust' placed before its possessor سپاه in compact poetic word order), so the whole means 'the army's dust rises up toward the sun.'
segments.s366-g152: نبیند کس is a negated-indefinite construction meaning 'no one sees' (verb نبیند + noun کس interpreted as 'no one' in context). از گرد تاریک راه is a prepositional phrase 'because of/from the dark dust/cloud of the road' (از = 'from/because of', گرد = 'dust/whirl', تاریک = 'dark', راه = 'road/path'), so 'no one sees the road because of the dark dust-cloud.'

sentences.s366-s16: By Zarir's life: that rider has not taken (him); by the precious life of Esfandiyar.

sentences.s366-s111: How could the tip of a dagger-like arrow reach that high sky, which stands (there) with a mountain of stone?
sentences.s366-s112: The wise man said to the king of the land, 'O noble‑hearted, eminent and praiseworthy one!'
sentences.s366-s57: Wherever that king turned his face, he rode on, ever seeking the blood of his ill-wishers.

sentences.s366-s60: He wept and went off wretched, and became abase; he roused Esfandiyār's steed.
sentences.s366-s101: He fell upon his face and became unconscious; he spoke no word either and became silent.

sentences.s366-s103: What can be said to me, O king? And when my day keeps turning, it will become dark.
sentences.s366-s104: Those who are more dear to me are the chosen of the host and are more famed.
sentences.s366-s93: He took to the desert with a small army; the king became victorious and the enemy was ruined.
sentences.s366-s94: Know this, O chosen king of kings: whatever I said is nothing other than that.
sentences.s366-s95: Let there be neither more nor less than this single saying; from now on do not turn a sullen face toward me.
sentences.s366-s96: For what I said I did not say except by your command, O victorious king.
